speaker: interviewer
text: The date is June 4, 1995. The survivor is Olga Kovacs. The interviewer is Dorothy Shiloff Simons in New York, New York, United States, and the language is English. So I should look at the--?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You can just repeat. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. My name is Dorothy Shiloff Simons. It's June 4, 1995. I'm doing an interview with Olga Kovacs. And in New York, New York, USA. Please state and spell your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Olga Kovacs. K-O-V-A-C-S. That's the Hungarian spelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever use any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And please tell us your date of birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Salgotarjan, Hungary, on May 23, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today is?

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell Salgotarjan for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-A-L-G-O-T-A-R-J-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'd like to begin by asking you to tell us something about your life before the war. Your childhood, about the town in which you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a family of 14. My parents, my grandparents, paternal grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have never known my maternal grandmother, and my grandfather-- paternal grandmother-- and my paternal grandfather passed away when I was a toddler. We always lived in Salgotarjan and my parents grew up in the town. I started schooling in Salgotarjan where my parents had a business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you to tell us the names of your parents and grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandparents were [? Cecilia ?] and [? Jacob ?] Biheller. B-I-H-E-L-L-E-R. My paternal grandparents were [? Louise ?] and [? Leopold ?] Kohn. K-O-H-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- my mother's first name is Sari-- S-A-R-I-- and my father's first name is Dezso-- D-E-Z-S-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kovacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandparents' last names, though, is different than your father's last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I tell you why. When the brothers-- father had two brothers-- went to college, then as far as I remember, being told by my father, the dean suggested to my grandfather that Mr. Kohn-- it would be a good idea if the children take up a Hungarian name. So the Kohn-- K-O-H-N-- thus became Kovacs, which in English means Smith.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did your parents have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father had two brothers. Uncle [? Bela ?] and [? Nandor. ?] Both were married. Eventually Uncle [? Nandor ?] divorced. He had a son-- my cousin George-- who died a number of years ago in Spain. And Uncle [? Bela's ?] wife-- Mucu-- M-U-C-U-- lives in Budapest with my cousin, Vera, her daughter, Susan, and two grandchildren, Gabor and Aniko-- A-N-I-K-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your father's-- this was your mother's--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's two brothers. My mother's two brothers were [? Bela, ?] who was married to Irene. They had a daughter, Edith. The other brother was Gino, married to Lilly and had a daughter, Agnes. Mother's sister Olga was married to Dr. Leslie-- or Laslow, Hungarian-- Laslow Schwabach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-C-H-W-A-B-A-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did they all live in Salgotarjan?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all lived in Salgotarjan, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you tell me about your childhood and your relations to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a most happy childhood filled with-- [CRYING] I'm sorry-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] filled with love and devotion. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see them for special occasions, or every day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or how?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Salgotarjan was a small town, so we had constant contact with each other. And we just adored each other. There was no dissension. And I remember, when-- I-- I hear this in America, that when there are more than two or three grandchildren there are sometimes favorites. I never felt that there are favorites. We were-- we were devoted to each other. And-- and the grandparents were devoted to us. And it was a most happy environment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewee
text: So did-- were you very close to your cousins? Yes. Yes, very good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you tell us something about your family's business and professions?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather had an undertaking, it was called Salgo Liquor and Rum Factory. This is spelled S-A-L-G-O. And there belonged wine and alcohol wholesale business, a wine vinegar manufacturing plant, some land, and liquor and-- distillery, what you really call in English. My grandfather-- my maternal grandfather-- had a hardware business, which upon their retirement-- the grandparents' retirement-- went to the two brothers. The sisters by then were married already. And then at the other hand, my paternal grandfather, upon his retirement, of course, left the business to my father and his two brothers. OK. And so how would you describe your status, economically and socially, in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we did not have any-- any-- any problems financially. No-- no problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You had everything you needed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how-- did you know how many people lived in the town? I believe there were about 22,000 people, out of which, probably-- and I really am not 1,000% sure about this-- but as far as I can remember-- and I might be wrong-- there were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a number of thousand Jewish people in town. Several thousand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several thousand Jewish people in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where in Hungary was Salgotarjan located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Salgotarjan is about 70 to 73 miles northeast from Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miles or kilometers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kilometers, kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to the Czechoslovak border of 1939. Very close, just a couple of kilometers from the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as far as you know, did your family live in that area for many generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I believe my maternal grandparents' grandparents came from northern Czechoslovakia, but everybody was born in Salgotarjan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family speak-- what languages did your family speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian, and the grandfather's house sometimes we spoke German, with a little Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little bit of Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Little bit of Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jews in Salgotarjan, did they basically speak Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Yiddish spoken at all, or just a little bit, like in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was an orthodox congregation, but in the strict sense, because, for example, when we went to the synagogue the women were sitting upstairs behind the-- not the bars, how do you call this in English? Behind the balustrade.

speaker: interviewer
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. And the men were downstairs. So there-- there-- there was also a segment of what you would call here Reformed Jewish entity, and the Orthodox entity.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was only one--

speaker: interviewee
text: One--

speaker: interviewer
text: --synegogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: One congregation, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us, then, about the Jewish life in the town, and your family's Jewish life. How would you describe your family's observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father-- every day, father was putting on the [NON-ENGLISH]. We had mezuzah on every door. We had a strict kosher household, and we duly observed Pesach. On the high holy days the business was closed, and father, along with his brothers, were praying in kittel. On Fridays-- on Saturdays our business was open. Grandfather's business closed in the middle of Friday afternoon and stayed closed during the whole day of Saturday. He went to the synagogue. He put on the [NON-ENGLISH] every day. They had the mezuzah on every single door. And he also wear the kittel in the-- during the-- the high holy days. And they always had the strictly kosher household, observing the Pesach, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this typical of the Jews in the town, this level of observance? Or were you considered more or less observant than others?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know, but-- but father and-- and grandfather felt that's how they want to do it, so they were doing it. I really don't have any idea how other people did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a formal Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Salgotarjan, in my time, there were not-- no Jewish schools. However, we had the rabbi, who taught us liturgy and history, the Jewish law, and-- and every single thing. But not in school. We had free time given by the school when we had to observe the religious education. But mostly at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the relations like between the Jews and the non-Jews in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It went all right for a while. But, for example, my mother told me that one of her best friend married-- after the First World War-- an officer. And when they came back after honeymoon to Salgotarjan and they met mother on the street-- they were very, very close friends-- she turned her head. So mother understood, and she-- she reacted also in a way that the person became a non-person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because-- and your mother thinks the reason for this--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, because she was Jewish, yes. And the officer, of course, the Hungarian officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you personally ever experience any-- any anti-Jewish behavior?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at the beginning. Not at the beginning. But gradually, like on Saturdays-- when I was still going to grammar school-- mother and grandfather used to pick me up, because it happen that the children-- and of course where do the children hear this? At home-- started mocking Jewish girls. We had a co-ed school system in Salgotarjan grammar school. And then I went to the gymnasium which is something different from what we have here, because a gymnasium here is where you-- exercises and all that, but in Hungary it was the equivalent of junior and senior high school. I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] attend four classes at the gymnasium, between age 10 and age 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would've been in--?

speaker: interviewee
text: '36 to '40. Gradually, we felt that there is some pressure in the gymnasium on Jewish girls. We were still not barred from that particular school, but my parents felt that I should follow my studies somewhere else. Then it happened, in 1940, that a friend of my parents came up with the idea that there was a French convent in Budapest called Notre Dame de Sion, that's-- who-- which is running the equivalent of a high school, plus language and secretarial school, in Budapest. And they would be more than willing to take new students. They couldn't have less cared about religion, per se, because they didn't even ask. We were not forced to 'convert,' quote, unquote-- nothing of that sort. Religion didn't even come to-- to be a problem. It was really an oasis during those times. I was staying with friends in Budapest--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, before we go on for you to tell us about that period I'd just like to go back a little bit and just ask a few more questions about your life before 1940. Was your family involved in any way in Zionist activity? Or was there Zionist activity in the town at all, besides the religious life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that. My parents were not involved in Zionism.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- they have-- I-- I remember there were-- there were meetings by the Keren Kayemeth, and there was another--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Keren Kayemeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, Keren Kayemeth, the Jewish National Fund.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish National Fund. And, of course, they were-- they were contributors to that. And then there-- they-- they had subscription to Zionist newspapers, but they were not active-- that's what I meant-- in the movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And a little more about your regular schooling in Salgotarjan. So you were attending-- the Jewish children attended the same schools as the non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it-- what-- can you tell us something about your school life and your friends, and anything that you can remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning of the grammar school-- from between six and ten-- there really was not anything-- anything too bad about it. But gradually, as the war broke out in-- the Second World War, in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, I meant about your-- just your daily life and what it was like growing up in the town, what your routine was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. And then my parents were rather strict about my homework, and I-- I tried to do my best. I probably should have done much more than I did. And I learned-- when I started at gymnasium I started also learning languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the gymnasium that was also co-ed, we started Latin the first year. And it was extremely difficult, and everybody hated it because of the grammar and all that. And then when I took up French, I was very grateful for the four years in Latin, because it resembles. And we had German. I had German from home. I started to talk German when I-- when I began to talk Hungarian. And in-- in Budapest I started to study French. But life in Salgotarjan was-- if I can say this-- it was easy for a child. First of all, the loving background at home-- it was-- it was like paradise, all of these loving relatives at home, and my grandparents, and the aunts, and uncles, and the cousins. And everybody rejoiced-- we rejoiced in each other as long as it lasted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us something about your mother. What did she do? Your father was in the business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Father was in business with the two brothers so mother-- mother stayed home and attended to the house. And as long as I-- as I lived with them, of course, supervised everything. Schooling and whatever I was involved in she-- she knew about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother was a marvelous friend. Not only a mother-- she was my best friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any of your aunts, and uncles, and cousins-- would you like to tell us something in particular about any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very good friends with them. I-- I really couldn't-- not even after all these years, there is absolutely nothing bad relating to the family that I could say. It-- it-- it was really one of those exceptional good relations with-- with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a house did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- You mean apartment-- room-wise?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was in an apartment house not far from the factories. And it was actually-- it-- it had a different layout, because when you-- when you walked in, you-- you were in a hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had the bathroom to the-- I can still see it, you know-- to the right, and the kitchen, and the pantry. In Hungary we had the pantry, not like a electric refrigerator like today. We had the little icebox with the ice, and the iceman came and started knocking the ice off of the big, you know-- how do you call this in English? The big-- Blocks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Block of ice, yeah. And then you went into the dining room. And to the left was my mother and my father's bedroom. To the right was my room. And then we had the sitting room next to that. It was a lovely apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Except a few pieces of china, nothing. Absolutely nothing. But that would have been the least because tangible-- to lose tangible things doesn't mean a thing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so in this period, during the '30s when you were growing up in Salgotarjan, you were telling us that you started to feel increased anti-Semitism in the town. What did you know, you and your family? What did you know about what was going on in Hungary at that time, and in Europe in general? Do you remember anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, in the late '30s, I believe, they-- Hungary was never a philo-Semitic country. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think there was something in the population that-- that's been always anti-Jewish. I'm not saying everybody. It would be very, very sad if everybody would be a villain in a nation of 10 millions. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gradually, especially when the Second World War-- even before the World War in the late '30s-- they have enacted the first anti-Jewish decrees-- anti-Jewish legislation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year--

speaker: interviewee
text: Government sponsored.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that on the university level the numerus clausus-- which meant the quota system, what we call here-- probably was enacted in the late '30s. And then, gradually, on the university level, it became the numerus nullus, which meant no Jews admitted. I cannot remember the exact date.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then when the Second World War broke out, on September 2, I believe, 1939, then, of course-- I must say of course-- Hungary sided with the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what knowledge did you have of what was going on, and your feelings at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt-- I felt this in school. In 1939, I attended the first-- my first year at the gymnasium. And I remember when the French were vanquished in the-- during spring-- I think it was in May or-- or April of 1940-- then one of my schoolmates-- I was in fourth grade and he was in eighth grade-- we are talking about the gymnasium-- he was extremely happy that the Germans were victorious over the French. However, I have to add that after the war he went around in Salgotarjan saying that ask Olga Kovacs how I felt during the war. And Olga Kovacs couldn't testify to that. Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you started to say that in 1940, because of this growing anti-Semitism in the school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your parents decided to send you to Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about how that decision was made, and what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The decision was made, as I mentioned earlier, that the school-- Notre Dame de Sion-- accepted Jewish girls. They did not even ask your religion. All they wanted was to teach, and I felt immediately accepted. And it-- it was like paradise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were other Jewish families doing things like this too in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends of ours. Friends of ours-- who-- who advised my parents about the possibility of-- of-- or suggestion that we-- we should try that-- had a daughter there. She-- she was a couple of years older than I. She already started the secretarial school. And the nuns were marvelous. They were just really superb.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel? Did you go with this friend, or she was already there?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was there. I was staying with friends of ours in Budapest. And I took the train-- it really was not the train. It's the tramway or the bus that you took in-- in Hungary from the apartment to go to school. It was not too far. I mean, Budapest compared to New York-- it-- it's really not such a big city. And I attended classes there. And I-- I-- I had classes every day. We had languages, history, literature-- everything. It was marvelous.
